title: Webs in type C
speaker: Benjamin Elias
affiliation: University of Oregon; von Neumann Fellow, School of Mathematics
abstract: In 1997, Kuperberg gave a generators-and-relations presentation of the monoidal category Fund, whose objects are tensor products of fundamental representations, for all rank 2 lie algebras. The general case has been an open problem since. It was solved in type A in 2014 by Cautis-Kamnitzer-Morrison, and this talk will be about the solution in type C, which is recent work of Bodish-Elias-Rose-Tatham. More importantly, we introduce a basis of our category, analogous to the basis constructed in type A by Elias in 2016.
tags: Workshop on Representation Theory and Geometry

proof_stages.label: Type C web presentation and theorem statement
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins by stating a generators-and-relations presentation for a pivotal monoidal web category intended to encode all morphisms among tensor products of fundamental representations of SP(2n), or its quantum group.

proof_stages.label: Type A web prototype and relation-sufficiency problem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews the GL_N/SL_N type A web category, where fundamental representations are exterior powers, trivalent vertices are wedge and co-wedge maps, and Morrison's combinatorial relations raise the central problem of proving that the listed relations are enough.

proof_stages.label: Skew Howe proof of the type A equivalence and its limitations
proof_stages.summary: The Cautis-Kamnitzer-Morrison proof is outlined as a skew Howe duality argument: lift an idempotented quantum group action to webs, ladderize arbitrary webs, identify the kernel, and thereby prove generic equivalence, while leaving integral and non-type-A extensions problematic.

proof_stages.label: Localized standard-representation and Hecke template
proof_stages.summary: After inverting the relevant small quantum numbers, one may reduce from all fundamental tensor products to tensor powers of the standard representation and use Schur-Weyl/Hecke algebra methods, braid reduction, and the known Hecke kernel as a template for the type C proof.

proof_stages.label: Type C setup, localization, and non-explicit web maps
proof_stages.summary: For type C, fundamental representations are symplectic summands rather than exterior powers and tilting forces the same localization; because explicit q-formulas for the generators are difficult, the coefficients and functor are determined indirectly from one-dimensional hom spaces, pivotal/ribbon data, and consistency constraints.

proof_stages.label: Brauer-BMW Schur-Weyl factorization and fullness
proof_stages.summary: The localized standard tensor powers are controlled by Brauer/BMW Schur-Weyl duality, and the Brauer/BMW category with crossings, cups, and caps factors through type C webs, implying fullness of the web functor to fundamental representations.

proof_stages.label: Kernel control, dimension bound, and integral Brauer variant
proof_stages.summary: Because the BMW kernel is known abstractly as a quantum Pfaffian but lacks an explicit diagrammatic formula, the proof uses web relations killing the forbidden n+1-labeled strand, Reidemeister/ladder-style reductions, and field dimension counts; a rotation-invariant four-valent variant is proposed as the route to an eventual integral presentation.

proof_stages.label: Cellular light-ladder basis and local intersection forms
proof_stages.summary: The planned longer proof will fill the integral gaps by giving explicit reduction formulas and a cellular light-ladder basis, whose minimal projection maps compute local intersection forms and connect the web presentation to modular representation theory and geometric Satake applications.

examples.name: Type A bigon coefficient as set-splitting count
examples.note: The bigon relation is explained by applying co-wedge then wedge to a basis vector and counting the ways to split a set, illustrating the combinatorial meaning of Morrison's web relations.

examples.name: One-step type A light ladder from omega_3 to omega_4
examples.note: For the standard GL_N representation, the weight omega_4 minus omega_3 has minimal dominant source omega_3, so the trivalent map L_3 tensor V to L_4 exemplifies a minimal projection used to generate light ladders.
examples.engagement_reason: he works through the minimal light-ladder case, computing μ=ω₄−ω₃, the dominance condition, λ=ω₃, λ+μ=ω₄, and the resulting trivalent map

examples.name: Geometric Satake interpretation via IC sheaves and convolution fibers
examples.note: In response to a question, local intersection forms are connected to compositions involving IC sheaves and intersections of cycles in fibers of convolution diagrams, illustrating the geometric Satake avatar of the same invariants.
